rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MARY L. MCFADDEN SURVIVOR'S TRUST, ESTABLISHED TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 5] Insofar as the argument for demurrer under section 430.10(e) is that Petitioner's legal assertions are incorrect or will not be successful, that determination is not within the scope of a demurrer, and those grounds for demurrer are OVERRULED. Demurrers test legal sufficiency based on the allegations as pleaded, without regard to the plaintiff's ability to prove the alleged facts. (see, Gray v. Dignity Health (2021) 70 Cal.App.5th 225.) A ruling on a demurrer does not determine contested factual issues relating to the merits. The Demurrer also argues the SAAP "is a trust contest that violates the no contest clause of the Third Amendment to the Mary L. McFadden Survivor's Trust." Determination of whether this argument will prevail is beyond the scope of a demurrer, which only tests the legal sufficiency of the pleadings, and does not determine their merits or viability. Demurrer pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e) is also brought on grounds that the claim is an untimely trust contest, filed beyond the 120-day period under Probate Code section 16061.7. Procedurally, this is an issue Patricia could have raised in the prior demurrer, but did not. It cannot be properly raised as grounds for demurrer now, in the successive demurrer. (Code of Civil Proc. sec. 430.41(b).) Substantively, the argument still does not support sustaining demurrer to the petition on these grounds. The Demurrer argues the SAAP is a trust contest and the time to file a contest to the Third Amendment to the to the Mary L. McFadden Survivor's Trust was "either 120 days after July 24, 2023 or 120 days after August 11, 2023[.]" (Notice at 5:13-14). The amended petition the SAAP further amends was filed September 25, 2023. On the face of the pleadings and the moving papers, demurrer to the SAAP on the basis of it being an untimely trust contest claim is OVERRULED. As the court stated in the previous order to the prior demurrer, while the argument that the Amendment to Amended Petition is materially an untimely trust contest might find traction in a different procedural posture, a demurrer is not the vehicle by which the court can make such a determination and ruling. Lastly, the Demurrer argues the SAAP fails to sufficiently allege the "impossibility" of the settlor complying with the Third Amendment to the Mary L. McFadden Survivor's Trust's requirements to validly amend the trust. The SAAP pleads at paragraph 6, "Margaret is informed and believes that, in view of the foregoing, Mary L.'s inability to obtain Michael Carroll's consent to the Fourth Amendment should be excused as the result of breaches of fiduciary duty by Michael Carroll, unforeseeable by Mary L., which constituted impossibility in view of all the relevant circumstances." (SAAP at 5:9-12). As stated in the prior order on demurrer, "impossibility of performance" is a common law defense to an action for failure to perform an obligation and has been codified in various California codes and discussed in various types of civil case law. There is no black letter test or series of elements established to determine "impossibility" in a given context or controversy, particularly in the probate trust context. The court previously sustained with leave to amend Patricia McFadden's demurrer to the prior amendment to amended petition, brought on a similar argument under section 430.10(e). That order's final paragraph states: The amendment shall be pleaded so as to present the factual basis for pleadings made on information and belief and to plead facts going to the necessary elements of the "impossibility" theory advanced by Margaret McFadden in the filings and opposition to the Demurrer, and to amend the Amended Prayer for Relief, paragraphs A, B, and C, accordingly. November 6, 2025 Order, at 5:16-19.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MARY L. MCFADDEN SURVIVOR'S TRUST, ESTABLISHED TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer To M. Mcfaddens Second Amendment To Amended Petition For Instructions Concerning Ascertaining The Beneficiaries And Trustee; Request For Appointment Of California Licensed Professional Fiduciary
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 5] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Russell S. Roeca. Before the court is Patricia McFadden's Demurrer to Margaret A. McFadden's Second Amendment To Amended Petition For Instructions Concerning Ascertaining The Beneficiaries And Trustee; Request For Appointment Of California Licensed Professional Fiduciary To Serve As Temporary Trustee Pending Decision ("Demurrer"). The Second Amendment To Amended Petition For Instructions Concerning Ascertaining The Beneficiaries And Trustee; Request For Appointment Of California Licensed Professional Fiduciary To Serve As Temporary Trustee Pending Decision ("SAAP") was filed November 24, 2025. The initial petition was filed September 8, 2023, and the Amended Petition the SAAP amends was filed September 25, 2023. Now, per the Notice of Demurrer, Patricia McFadden demurs to the SAAP on several grounds: [T]this demurrer is brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10, subdivisions (a) (b)(d)-(g) [demurrer to complaint for failure of capacity to sue, failure to state a claim, misjoinder, failure to state a cause of action, uncertainty and unascertainable contract], as well as section 430.20, subdivisions (a)-(c) [demurrer to answer for failure to state a claim, uncertainty and unascertainable contract]. Moreover, the TAP is a trust contest that violates the no contest clause of the Third Amendment to the Mary L. McFadden Survivor's Trust and that violates the statute of limitation to file a trust contest (Prob. Code [sec.] 16061.7). Thus, the TAP fails to state a claim, and Margaret is without legal capacity to sue. Notice at 2:16-24. A demurrer is a pleading used to challenge the legal sufficiency of an opponent's pleading, based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318; County of Fresno v. Shelton (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 996, 1008, 1009.) When considering a demurrer, the trial court must accept as true all material facts pleaded in the complaint and those arising by reasonable implication. (Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal. App. 4th 784.; Sheehan v. San Francisco 49ers, Ltd. (2009) Cal.4th 992, 998.) Demurrer to the SAAP on grounds under Code of Civil Procedure section 430.20, which is for objections by demurrer to an answer, is OVERRULED. The pleading now subject to the Demurrer is a petition brought pursuant to the Probate Code seeking affirmative relief. It is not an "answer" subject to demurrer under Code of Civil Procedure section 430.20 (see, Code of Civil Proc. sec. 431.30; Mechling v. Asbestos Defendants (2018) 29 CA5th 1241, 1248).

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MARY L. MCFADDEN SURVIVOR'S TRUST, ESTABLISHED TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 5] Demurrer to the SAAP pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(f), "The pleading is uncertain" is OVERRULED. As used in section 430.10(f), "uncertain" includes ambiguous and unintelligible. Uncertainty is the broad ground for demurrer, that covers any defective statement making the cause of action or any part of it unclear. Ambiguity is narrower, available when the complaint is doubtful in meaning. A complaint is unintelligible when its meaning cannot be comprehended at all (see, e.g., Craig v. Los Angeles (1941) 44 C.A.2d 71, 73). The SAAP is the second amendment to an amended petition. The SAAP facially states "this Second Amendment incorporates the allegations of the Amended Petition without modification, including definitions and Exhibits." (SAAP at 5:4-5.) The SAAP is not the entire pleading before the court; it inserts the section "New Factual Allegations" into the existing amended pleading. This form of pleading, with one document amending but not fully replacing a previously filed amended petition, has evidently caused confusion as to what Margaret is currently alleging or seeking through this petition. The court notes, when it ordered Margaret to file an amended petition following the prior demurrer being sustained with leave to amend, the court expected a single, cohesive new amended pleading to be filed, not a further amendment to the amended petition filed in 2023. Regardless, when read as an amended portion of the amended petition, the newly pleaded allegations and prayer for relief in the SAAP plead claims clearly enough for Patrica to argue in the Demurrer that Margaret will be unable to prove them (see, e.g., Butler v. Sequeira (1950) 100 C.A.2d 143, 145-146). Demurrer to SAAP under section 430.10(f) is OVERRULED. Demurrer to the SAAP pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(g), "In an action founded upon a contract, it cannot be ascertained from the pleading whether the contract is written, is oral, or is implied by conduct," is OVERRULED. On the face of the pleading, the SAAP's claims for relief are not founded upon a contract and do not facially plead or allege breach of a contract. Demurrer to the SAAP pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e), "The pleading does not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action," is brought on several grounds. When a demurrer challenges the sufficiency of the cause of action pleaded, as the Demurrer does here, the issue before the court is whether the facts pleaded state a valid cause of action - any cause of action. It is error for a trial court to sustain a general demurrer when the pleading has stated a cause of action under any possible legal theory. The inquiry on a demurrer is not whether the facts pleaded are independently verifiable, not whether the petitioner is legally entitled to the relief demanded, and not whether the legal theory is sound. (See, e.g. Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal. App. 4th 784.; Sheehan v. San Francisco 49ers, Ltd. (2009) Cal.4th 992, 998, Hacker v. Homeward Residential, Inc. (2018) 26 Cal.App.5th 270, 280; Venice Town Council, Inc. v. City of Los Angeles (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 1547, 1561-1562; Quelimane Co., Inc. v. Stewart Title Guaranty Co. (1998) 19 Cal.4th 26, 38-39; New Livable Calif. v. Association of Bay Area Governments (2020) 59 CA5th 709, 714-715,; Adelman v. Associated Int'l Ins. Co. (2001) 90 CA4th 352, 359.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MARY L. MCFADDEN SURVIVOR'S TRUST, ESTABLISHED TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 5 of 5] The SAAP has complied with the court's direction in this regard, pleading the factual basis for allegations previously pleaded only on information and belief and pleading additional facts going to the "impossibility" theory that the settlor could not comply with the Third Amendment's requirements of valid trust amendment due to the alleged actions of others, and thus the Fourth Amendment should be the controlling trust amendment. Pleadings must contain "ultimate facts," rather than evidentiary facts or conclusions of law, to survive a demurrer under Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e). The "ultimate facts" pleading standard requires factual particularity in pleading the essential elements of a legal claim, sufficient to acquaint a defendant with the nature, source and extent of the cause of action, but does not require the plaintiff to plead the specific evidence she will use to prove those elements. (Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal.4th 531, 550.) The California Supreme Court in Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal. 4th 531, 550 established that a plaintiff may allege on information and belief any matters that are not within her personal knowledge, if she has information leading her to believe that the allegations are true. Whether Margaret could have pleaded additional or different facts in support of the theory and in anticipation of Patricia's opposition, or whether this theory will ultimately prevail, is not the inquiry on a demurrer or grounds for the court to sustain demurrer to a cause of action otherwise sufficiently pleaded. Although Margaret's petition has survived the Demurrer, the court urges the parties to remember: a pleading surviving a demurrer to the sufficiency of the pleadings indicates only that the court could discern any cognizable cause of action from the face of the pleadings, after treating all properly pleaded facts as true. It is not an indication of the merits of the petition. The court notes again, as it did in the prior order on demurrer, the Petition appears to be substantively a trust contest pleaded so as to avoid the consequences of a trust contest under the Third Amendment's no contest clause and/or a statute of limitations. The court is concerned that the evidence referenced in the amended pleadings which allegedly supports the "impossibility" theory, in actuality goes to a fraud or undue influence cause of action. Margaret McFadden shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MARY L. MCFADDEN SURVIVOR'S TRUST, ESTABLISHED TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 5] Demurrer to elements of the SAAP's prayer, discussed in the MPA at page 8, is OVERRULED. A demurrer tests the sufficiency of the factual allegations of the complaint rather than the relief sought in the prayer of the complaint. A defective prayer may be properly subject to a motion to strike, but is irrelevant to whether a cause of action is sufficiently stated for the purpose of a demurrer. (See, e.g., Venice Town Council, Inc. v. City of Los Angeles (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 1547, 1562; City of King City v. Community Bank of Central California (2005) 131 Cal.App.4th 913, fn. 3; Gomez v. Volkswagen of America, Inc. (1985) 169 Cal.App.3d 921, 925; Grieves v. Superior Court (1984) 157 Cal.App.3d 159, 166, fn. 9). Demurrer to the SAAP pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(a), "The court has no jurisdiction of the subject of the cause of action alleged in the pleading," is OVERRULED. The underlying amended petition facially pleads the factual basis for this court to have subject matter jurisdiction over this matter pursuant to Probate Code sections 17200. Demurrer to the SAAP pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(b), "The person who filed the pleading does not have the legal capacity to sue," is OVERRULED. Per the SAPP, it is brought by Margaret McFadden as "appointed successor trustee" of one of the amended trusts in dispute (SAAP at 1:21). Facially, the SAAP is not brought on behalf of the deceased settlor, Mary L. McFadden. Demurrer to the SAAP pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(d), "There is a defect or misjoinder of parties," in that unnamed but referenced family members and Michael Carroll are not named as respondents, is OVERRRULED. Procedurally, this is an issue Patricia could have raised in the prior demurrer, but did not. It cannot be raised as grounds for demurrer now, in the successive demurrer. (Code of Civil Proc. sec. 430.41(b).) Substantively, pursuant to Probate Code sections 48 and 1043, interested persons may respond or object to trust petitions (see also, e.g., Colvis v. Binswanger (2023) 96 Cal.App.5th 393). Probate Code section 17203 establishes comprehensive notice requirements for trustees, beneficiaries, and any other person whose rights would be affected by the petition, but this is a procedural notice requirement, not a requirement for a petition to name those parties as respondents. There is no specific statutory requirement that a petition name respondent.
